original_abstract: This article provides a critical examination of the concept of illiberalism, aiming to refine its definition for better application in empirical research. Moving beyond existing frameworks that often link the term exclusively to modern democratic backsliding or specific right-wing ideologies, the author proposes a structural definition centered on the rejection of three core liberal democratic pillars: limited executive power, state neutrality (impartiality), and the open society (universalism and individual autonomy). By decoupling the concept from specific institutional regimes or narrow temporal scopes, the study identifies illiberalism as a diverse ideational syndrome rather than a singular ideology. The second half of the paper delineates the conceptual boundaries between illiberalism, populism, and authoritarianism, and introduces a comprehensive typology comprising nine distinct "routes" to illiberalism: populist, authoritarian, traditionalist, religious, paternalist, libertarian, nativist-nationalist, materialist-technocratic, and left-wing. This multidimensional approach offers a more flexible and globally applicable toolkit for analyzing the various ways in which liberal democratic norms are currently being challenged.
humanized_abstract: This article tries to understand the concept of illiberalism with the aim of rendering it more operational for empirical research. The term has been used in an inconsistent way, often simply identifying it with forms of modern democratic regression or with right-wing politics. Focusing on the underlying liberal principles of limited state power, state neutrality and an open society, this paper defines illiberalism as the ideological rejection of these principles, thereby turning it into a more generic concept applicable to a broader set of contexts and ideas. This makes illiberalism into a diversum — syndrome of ideas — rather than an ideology. The second part of the paper tries to provide more details regarding the concepts surrounding illiberalism, i.e. populism and authoritarianism and presents a broader typology, where nine possible routes to illiberalism are identified: populist, authoritarian, traditionalist, religious, paternalist, libertarian, nativist-nationalist, materialist-technocratic and left-wing.
